message: 220 human-human dialogs.

Intent is annotated with a hierarchical scheme that first separates on-task and off-task intents, then defines task-specific on-task intents and universal off-task dialog-act categories. The off-task intents are shared across tasks and include six general intents and six social intents, and annotation is done at the sentence level after segmenting each conversation turn into single sentences.
TransferTransfo and the hybrid model.

The model is evaluated with automatic metrics: Perplexity (PPL), Response-Intent Prediction (RIP), Response-Slot Prediction (RSP), Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP), and Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP), where RIP/RSP compare predicted response intents/slots against human-annotated ground truth, and ERIP/ERSP additionally credit acceptable intent/slot alternatives using transition probabilities from training data. It is also evaluated with human metrics: Fluency, Coherence, Engagement, Dialog Length, and Task Success Score, where task success is the average types of attacker information obtained (name, address, phone number).

OpenTapioca is not state-of-the-art overall: its reported F1 scores are 0.482/0.399 on AIDA-CoNLL, 0.087/0.515 on Microposts 2016, 0.870/0.858 on ISTEX-1000, and 0.335/0.310 on RSS-500 (micro/macro). Compared to the best reported systems, it is much worse on AIDA-CoNLL and RSS-500, mixed on Microposts 2016, but best on ISTEX-1000.

They compare against two state-of-the-art early rumour detection baselines: Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012). Both use various message-, user-, topic-, and propagation-based features with an SVM classifier.
Accuracy and the standard TDT evaluation procedure using Detection Error Trade-off (DET) curves. They also measure efficiency by throughput per second.

No. They frame rumour detection as a classification problem and learn the weight vector and threshold on training data, using SVM training including a two-round training procedure for pseudo feedback features.
Yes. They created their own dataset, including 202 confirmed rumours from Sina Weibo’s official rumour debunking service and 202 non-rumours.
Chinese.

They consider rumours to be unconfirmed or novel information with respect to trusted resources: “information not confirmed by official news is an indication of rumours” and “Unconfirmed (novel) information with respect to trusted resources is considered as an indication of rumours.”
They compared against LDA, Doc-NADE, HTMM, and GMNTM.
The paper explores generative document evaluation, document classification, and the topic2sentence task. It also includes qualitative inspection by examining sentences generated under each topic.
CoNLL2003, OntoNotes 5.0, Chinese OntoNotes 4.0, MSRA, Weibo NER, and Resume NER.

They incorporate direction and relative distance by adding a relative positional encoding \(R_{t-j}\), defined from the offset \(t-j\), directly into the attention score: \(A^{rel}_{t,j}=Q_tK_j^T+Q_tR_{t-j}^T+uK_j^T+vR_{t-j}^T\). Because \(R_{t-j}\) uses sinusoidal terms where the cosine parts are the same for forward/backward offsets but the sine parts change sign, the attention can distinguish both distance and direction.

Yes. The paper states that TENER achieves superior performance than prevailing BiLSTM-based models, and on English NER it “unveil[s] the new state-of-the-art performance” on two datasets under the non-contextualized embedding setting.
The state-of-the-art systems are SEMAFOR, SLING, and Stanford KBP system.

The evaluation used two datasets for KALM-QA: a manually constructed set of general questions based on the 50 logical frames, and the MetaQA dataset. The MetaQA dataset contains almost 29,000 test questions and over 260,000 training questions.

The paper contributes: (i) an adaptation of a state-of-the-art seq2seq neural system to Italian event detection and classification in a single-step approach; (ii) an investigation of the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task; and (iii) a comparison against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier. It also makes the pre-trained models and scripts publicly available.

The paper uses FBK-HLT as the baseline system. FBK-HLT is the best system from EVENTI Subtask B and is a cascade of two SVM classifiers, one for detection and one for classification, based on rich linguistic features.
Unanswerable

They assign each cluster word to a WordNet synset by choosing the set of synsets that minimizes the total distance between synsets, then generate candidate labels as the union of all hypernyms of those chosen synsets. They decide the semantic concept label by ranking these candidate labels using the sum of their WordNet pathwise distances to all chosen synsets, selecting labels that are least distant overall.

They discover coherent word clusters by first training domain-specific Word2Vec CBOW embeddings and then applying k-means clustering to the embeddings of gender-associated words, running k-means 50 times and keeping the model with the lowest sum of squared errors. They then use WordNet plus the domain-specific embeddings to disambiguate senses, generate candidate labels from hypernyms of the chosen synsets, and rank those labels by their summed WordNet pathwise distance to the cluster synsets.
The paper introduces two datasets, each consisting of over 300K sentences.
The strong baseline label was the centroid of the cluster. There was also a random baseline of 20% for the Word Intrusion task.
They generally rank low and often produce inappropriate responses such as flirtatious or retaliatory replies rather than polite refusal. Only a few data-driven systems ever politely refuse.
9960 HITs from 472 crowd workers. After filtering, 18,826 scores remained.

Yes. The automatically annotated time expressions were evaluated: the customised HeidelTime version used for automatic annotation achieves an F1 value of 89.1 for partial identification and normalization.
Agglutinative and fusional languages.
Yes. The paper explicitly mentions Turkish data with derivational boundaries and inflectional groups, and the morphology-level model uses morphological analysis outputs that include such tags.

Gold morphological features are used: the model uses outputs of morphological analysis consisting of the token’s lemma followed by language-specific morphological tags. For some languages, additional information is included, such as part-of-speech tags for Turkish.

The paper uses Semantic Scholar (SemScholar), Springer Nature’s SciGraph, the Textbook Question Answering (TQA) corpus, and the January 2018 English Wikipedia dataset. It also uses Flickr30K and COCO as image-sentence matching benchmarks.
Unanswerable
The ad-hoc approaches explored are the TQA baselines from Kembhavi et al. (2017): random, BiDAF, text only (TQA1, based on MemoryNet), text+image (TQA2, VQA), and text+diagrams (TQA3, DSDP-NET).
The supervised baselines were the five TQA baselines from Kembhavi et al. (2017): random, BiDAF, text only (TQA1, based on MemoryNet), text+image (TQA2, VQA), and text+diagrams (TQA3, DSDP-NET).

Yes. The main data is scientific-domain specific: SemScholar and SciGraph are corpora of scientific publications, and the TQA corpus is from middle school science curricula; only Wikipedia is described as general-purpose rather than scientific-domain specific.

They come from scientific publications in the Semantic Scholar corpus: the authors downloaded 3.3M PDFs and extracted 12.5M figures and captions using PDFFigures2. They also used an 80K-paper intersection of Springer Nature’s SciGraph with Semantic Scholar, yielding 82K figures.
Unanswerable
No, they explicitly state that their model predicts intensity “without leveraging on lexicons.”
The baseline was the Weka baseline.

Their model outperformed the Weka baseline on the development set but underperformed it on the test set. The best results in Table 2 were average Pearson correlations of 0.689 on development and 0.522 on test for EmoAtt, versus 0.611 and 0.648 for the baseline.

The training, validation, and test datasets provided for the EmoInt-2017 shared task were used, consisting of tweets annotated with Best-Worst Scaling for four emotions: joy, sadness, fear, and anger.

They do not give a single formal definition of hate speech; instead, they describe it as content that “promotes the dehumanization of people who are typically from minority groups” and “can incite hate crime.” Their annotation scheme further treats it through categories such as offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal.
English, French, and Arabic.

The paper considers five aspects: whether the text is direct or indirect; whether it is offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal; the attribute on which it discriminates; the name of the targeted group; and how annotators feel about its content on a negative-to-neutral sentiment range.

The dataset contains a 100-tweet pilot corpus per language, plus 5,647 English tweets, 4,014 French tweets, and 3,353 Arabic tweets. In total, that is 13,314 tweets including the pilot data, or 13,014 in the final comparable corpora.

Semantic Web technologies offer opportunities in MT by supporting semantic and syntactic disambiguation, improving named entity recognition and translation, adapting translation to non-standard speech using user/context knowledge, and enabling the translation and enrichment of knowledge bases for use in MT models. They can exploit structured knowledge, semantic annotations, SPARQL queries, linked data resources such as DBpedia, ontologies, reasoning, and multilingual knowledge graphs to improve translation quality, especially for ambiguous terms, entities, idioms, and domain-specific expressions. Recent work cited in the document also reports promising gains when integrating entity linking, knowledge graph embeddings, and translated KBs into MT.

Semantic Web technologies in Machine Translation are associated with challenges such as complex semantic ambiguity, structural divergence, and difficult linguistic features like tense and grammatical gender, as well as low-resource language pairs, domain adaptation, non-standard speech, and translations among morphologically rich languages. Although Semantic Web technologies can help with disambiguation and named entities, they are tedious to implement for common words, have not yet been fully exploited across multiple resources, and reasoning-based solutions suffer from poor run-time performance. The combination of Semantic Web and Machine Translation is still in its infancy.

Complex semantic ambiguity, structural divergence, and linguistic properties/features are other obstacles to automatic translation not mentioned in the abstract. The paper also lists excessive focus on English/European languages and low-resource pairs, cross-domain SMT limitations, translating non-standard social network text, difficult translation among morphologically rich languages, and mismatch between speech-translation training data and real user speech.

The EEG features used were five statistical features: root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis, and power spectral entropy. In total, this gave 31 channels × 5 features = 155 EEG features.

The baselines were end-to-end ASR systems using an attention model and a CTC model. They were evaluated with EEG features alone and with concatenated EEG and acoustic features, and also compared against using only T7 and T8 EEG sensors versus all 31 sensors with dimension reduction.

The dataset used was the first 30 sentences from the USC-TIMIT database. The authors built two simultaneous speech-EEG recording datasets, where subjects spoke these USC-TIMIT sentences under 40 dB noise (dataset A) and 65 dB background music noise (dataset B).
No. In Table 3, all LadaBERT variants have lower accuracy than the teacher BERT-Base on every reported task.

Yes. They compare weight pruning, matrix factorization, and hybrid pruning in Section 4.3 and conclude in Section 4.5 that combining matrix factorization with weight pruning is better than single solutions.
MNLI-m, MNLI-mm, SST-2, QQP, and QNLI.
DailyMail news.
They use two datasets created from DailyMail news for training, and evaluate on the DUC-2002 single-document summarization task and on 500 articles from the DailyMail test set.
Yes. They include a neural abstractive summarization baseline (“NN-ABS”) in their system comparisons and report its ROUGE results on DUC-2002 and DailyMail.

They are talking about commonsense ontologies, specifically hierarchical transitive entailment relations such as IS-A relations. The paper also mentions augmenting this with non-hierarchical knowledge from raw text.

They mean the embedding space is structured so that its geometry directly encodes the ontology’s partial-order constraints. In their order-embedding model, a subcategory vector is greater than or equal to its supercategory vector in every coordinate, so more general concepts are smaller than more specific ones in each dimension.
Yes. They used pretrained sentiment classifiers, including a character-level multiplicative LSTM trained on 82 million Amazon reviews and a recently released target-based sentiment analysis model.

The models extracted word unigrams and bigrams represented with TF-IDF, distributed semantic features from 300-dimensional word2vec embeddings averaged over each segment, and sentiment features from Empath’s 192 lexical categories plus pre-trained sentiment embeddings learned with a biLSTM on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank. They also experimented with GloVe and Sent2Vec representations.
Unanswerable
Grouping, connectivity, and discourse structure.

The crowd-sourced annotators are described as non-expert Amazon Mechanical Turk workers, and the expert annotators as trained experts. The document does not state that the annotators were familiar with the science topics annotated.

They are compared through two experiments that evaluate how well graph neural networks classify diagram parts and entire diagrams using graph-based representations from the crowd-sourced AI2D annotations and the expert AI2D-RST annotations. Performance is compared using accuracy, macro F1, and weighted F1 scores averaged over 20 runs, with statistical significance tested using the Mann–Whitney U test.
Amazon Mechanical Turk.
The trained experts are the five annotators who annotated the AI2D-RST subset of 1000 diagrams using the new multi-layer annotation schema.

They use two neural model architectures: a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a Recurrent Neural Network in the form of a Hierarchical Attention Network (HAN). Overall, the HAN appears to be their preferred architecture, as it most often achieves the best balanced and robust results.

They use the Clueweb09-derived dataset of [4] for baseline comparison, and for cross-temporal, cross-topic, and cross-domain training/evaluation they generate a new dataset based on Wikipedia crawl data. This new dataset is built from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” pages from 2018 and 2009.
Cross-domain. It has the lowest reported F1 scores overall compared with cross-topic and cross-temporal results, while the annotator analysis is not presented as a harder setup.

They use a Wikipedia-crawl-derived weak signal dataset: controversial seeds come from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” pages from 2018 and 2009, and they iteratively crawl “See also,” “References,” and “External links” up to two hops. Non-controversial seeds come from Wikipedia’s random article endpoint, and linked pages inherit the controversy label.
Yes. They compare the neural semantic approaches against lexical/full-text baselines such as TfIdf-SVM and language models, as well as other prior methods.
About 64 million segments from YouTube videos, comprising a total of 1.2 billion tokens, were used for training.
About 64 million segments from YouTube videos, comprising a total of 1.2 billion tokens.
The video features were 1500-dimensional frame-level vectors extracted from the video frames at 1-second intervals, similar to features used for large-scale image classification tasks.

Yes. The experiments used an LSTM-based NMT architecture “implementing the attention mechanism,” specifically the “scaled Luong” attention mechanism, and they also experimented with a Transformer model.

An established neural machine translation architecture based on LSTMs with attention was explored, specifically the Luong et al. (2017) model. They also experimented with the Transformer model, but its results were worse.

Symbolic rewriting is rewriting symbolic terms from one form to another according to rewrite rules, such as applying an equation \(s=t\) to replace \(u[\theta(s)]\) with \(u[\theta(t)]\). In this paper, examples include pairs of terms before and after such rewriting, or arbitrary polynomial terms paired with their normalized forms.

They incorporate expert knowledge by having users specify anchor terms/variables that encode domain knowledge, and adding a mutual-information term between those anchors and latent factors to the CorEx objective. This preserves information about the anchors while still explaining dependencies in the data, allowing anchors to seed topics and impose semantics on latent factors.
20 Newsgroups and the i2b2 Obesity Challenge 2008 corpus.
No. In the reported results, they compare Anchored CorEx to Unsupervised CorEx and a Naive Bayes baseline, not to popular topic models such as LDA.
Unanswerable

Peng and Dredze (2016) is the state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media. The paper states that its integrated training model (F-Score Driven Model II) achieves a new state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media.
Sina Weibo service in China.
A modified labeled Weibo NER corpus, along with the same unlabeled Sina Weibo text used by Peng and Dredze (2016).
XNLI (cross-lingual natural language inference), MLDoc (document classification), cross-lingual intent classification, POS tagging, and named entity recognition.
Yes. The document states that the mNMT model performs worse than the bilingual baseline on high-resource language pairs but improves upon it on low-resource language pairs.
No. The paper states that mBERT is trained on monolingual data in 104 languages, and reports a setup where the classifier is trained on English only and evaluated on all 15 languages.

They did not select a fixed set of 50 languages; the experiments span over 50 languages across 5 tasks. For POS tagging, the 48 languages were chosen as the intersection of languages with POS labels in Universal Dependencies v2.3 and the languages supported by their mNMT model.

They use both automatic and human evaluations to evaluate dialogue. Automatic evaluation uses perplexity and BLEU against human-annotated responses, while human evaluation uses ACUTE-EVAL self-chat pairwise comparisons of full dialogues on engagingness, interestingness, and humanness.
A significant gap exists between the cross-lingual model and the other models. In human evaluation, M-CausalBert achieves nearly a 100 percent winning rate against XNLG.

They compare against translation-pipeline models that use a two-step translation: from any language to English before model inference, and from English back to the target language after inference. In human evaluation, this translation-based system is the Poly-encoder (Poly).
Chinese, French, Indonesian, Italian, Korean, and Japanese.
Yes. They automatically created their own contrastive test set from the OpenSubtitles corpus by extracting examples and introducing contrastive translations.

The baselines are standard context-agnostic sentence-level models: for the recurrent setting, a bidirectional RNN with attention trained with Nematus, and for the Transformer setting, a standard Transformer-base model. Both do not use any additional context.

The context-aware models experimented were, for RNNs: concat22, s-hier, s-t-hier, and s-hier-to-2; and for Transformers: concat22, concat21, and the Voita et al. (2018) model. The paper also mentions testing weight tying variants for the recurrent models.
English and German. They train on WMT 2017 English→German news translation data and evaluate translation of the English pronoun “it” into German pronouns “es”, “sie”, and “er”.

They use a simple entity linking algorithm that directly links a mention to the entity with the greatest commonness score, where commonness is computed from Wikipedia anchor links and estimates the probability of an entity given the mention string. They also use this commonness score as the confidence of the linking result, and within the same document they apply a heuristic to corefer generic person mentions to more specific mentions.

They use a BiLSTM-based architecture: the sentence with a special mention token is encoded by two layers of BiLSTMs for context representation. This is combined with an averaged mention-string embedding, a KB type one-hot vector from entity linking, and the linking score, then fed into a 3-layer MLP for prediction.
FIGER (GOLD) and BBN. OntoNotes is mentioned but not used.
F1-score.
The fine-tuned BERT model gives an F1-score of 0.89 on the concept recognition dataset, using an 80-20 train-evaluation split.
Unanswerable

The labeling scheme is a BIO labeling scheme with 11 entity types: abb, grp, syscon, opcon, seterm, event, org, art, cardinal, loc, and mea. These represent abbreviations; groups/individuals; system concepts; operational concepts; generic SE terms; events; organizations; artifacts/instruments; numerical values; locations; and measures/features/behaviors, respectively.
BERT’s pre-trained cased language model.
The constructed dataset contains roughly 3700 annotated sentences at the word-token level.
Precision, recall and F-measure were computed.
The hand-crafted features mentioned are the position of sentence, sentence length, tense, content features, qualifying adjectives, and meta-discourse features.
The paper uses word2vec embeddings. Specifically, it uses AVGWVEC and PARAVEC embeddings, trained on ACL+AZ, MixedAbs, BSWE, and Brown corpus data, with feature dimensions of 100 or 300.
No. They use existing corpora: the ACL Anthology Reference Corpus and MixedAbs for training, and the already AZ-annotated Argumentative Zoning Corpus for testing.

Sentence embeddings were generated using three models: by averaging the word vectors in a sentence, by learning paragraph vectors (PV-DM/Paragraph Vector), and by training BAS-specific word vectors and then averaging them in the sentence. In the averaging model, the sentence vector is the average of the word embeddings learned by the classical word2vec algorithm from a large text corpus.

Argumentative zoning is rhetorical sentence classification: a process of assigning rhetorical status to extracted sentences. It provides readers with general discourse context so that scientific ideas can be better linked, compared, and analyzed.

They crawled two credit-based freemium blackmarket sites, YouLikeHits and Like4Like, from February to April 2019, created dummy accounts, and recorded the Tweet IDs of tweets posted there for gaining retweets. They then used Twitter’s REST API to collect the tweet objects, and also collected the authors’ timelines to obtain genuine tweets by the same users.

They compare against state-of-the-art Twitter spam detection methods and feature-concatenation baselines. Specifically, the baselines are Wu et al.’s Twitter spam detection method, Rajdev et al.’s approach, and classifiers trained on concatenated features using Random Forest, MLP, and SVM.
English

They mainly look at freemium blackmarket services, specifically three categories: social-share services, credit-based services, and auto-time retweet services. They also mention the broader blackmarket models premium and freemium.

They use English and Chinese for the two-language pretraining setting, and English, French, and Chinese for the three-language pretraining setting. The first pretraining stage is initialized from a 15-language pre-trained XLM.

The decoder is based on Transformer in a sequence-to-sequence model and supports multiple languages. It generates the output sequence in the target language by conditioning on the encoder’s vector representations and a specific language tag.

The encoder is part of a Transformer-based sequence-to-sequence model, and it is bidirectional. It takes a sentence together with its language tag embedding and encodes multilingual input into vector representations in a shared embedding space.

XLM is their main baseline. They also use pipeline baselines based on XLM that translate inputs/outputs for cross-lingual settings, and for supervised English-English question generation they additionally compare against CorefNQG and MP-GSN.

Triple pairing task accuracy on Amazon Mechanical Turk, where judges select the correct prompt-story pairing for groups of three stories. They also use human preference judgments in a blind test comparing hierarchical generation against generation without a prompt.
Perplexity and prompt ranking accuracy.

Language model baselines: non-hierarchical story generation models that do not condition on the prompt, using the gated convolutional language model (GCNN) of Dauphin et al. (2017) plus their self-attention mechanism. They also compare against seq2seq baselines using LSTMs, convolutional seq2seq, and Conv seq2seq with decoder self-attention; an ensemble of two Conv seq2seq with self-attention models; and a KNN baseline that retrieves the closest training prompt using TF-IDF vectors built with FASTTEXT and searched with FAISS.
No. The paper mentions training a fusion model on top of a pre-trained seq2seq model, but does not mention using pre-trained embeddings such as BERT.
The premise is generated using the convolutional language model from Dauphin et al. (2017).
Yes. The dataset is collected from Reddit’s Writing Prompts forum, where users submit story premises and other users respond by writing stories.
From Reddit’s Writing Prompts forum. They scraped three years of prompts and their associated stories using the official Reddit API.
word2vec, fastText, GloVe, Baroni, and SL999.

SICK, STSB, and MRPC were used. Specifically, SICK was used for textual entailment recognition and also for semantic textual similarity, STSB for semantic textual similarity, and Microsoft Research Paraphrase Corpus (MRPC) for paraphrase identification.
DT TEAM, ECNU, BIT, TF-KLD, Multi-Perspective CNN, InferSent, GRAN, Paragram-Phrase, and HCTI.
Fleiss’s Kappa.
Yes. They report inter-annotator agreement in Table 3 for the labeled Twitter data and state that they computed reliability using Fleiss’s Kappa.
170 workers participated.

Neighborhood overlap features are used: downward overlap, upward overlap, inward overlap, outward overlap, and bidirectional overlap between the author and target in the directed following network. They also use basic user account metrics from author and target profiles: number of friends, number of followers, verified status, and number of tweets posted within six-month timeline snapshots.
aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance

Cyberbullying is defined in the document as containing a strict subset of these criteria: aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance. It is also discussed as related to traditional bullying, which Olweus defines by repetition, harmful intent, and an imbalance of power.

They evaluated the output objectively for similarity to the training data using Phrase Overlap and 4-gram Jaccard similarity, syntactically using the Link Grammar Parser to measure valid linkages and sentence correctness, and subjectively for humor/content quality using human judgment. They also reported syntactic accuracy as the percentage of sentences with at least one valid linkage at Null Count 0.
The joke data came from two sources: CrowdTruth and Subreddits.
Inspirational quotes.
1 × 3 filters.

On FB15k-237, they report outperforming the previous state-of-the-art by 0.105 MRR, which is about 25.1% relative improvement, and by 6.1% absolute in Hits@10. On WN18RR, they state that CapsE obtains the best MR score.
No, the experiments only report using BERT-based models and BERT with added task-specific architectures such as Bi-LSTM, DenseNet, HighwayLSTM, BIMPM, and Sim-Transformer.

They compared BERT with several variants: BERT + Bi-LSTM for sequence labeling; BERT + HighwayLSTM, BERT + DenseNet, and an ensembled model for text classification; and BERT + BIMPM, BERT + BIMPM with the first bi-LSTM removed, and BERT + Sim-Transformer for semantic similarity.
CoNLL03, Yahoo Answer Classification Dataset, and Quora-Question-Pair dataset.

Cohen’s kappa averaged over three pairwise comparisons was .90/.93/.88 for scene role, function, and full construal in Phase 1, and .92/.88/.88 in Phase 2. Raw agreement was .92/.95/.90 in Phase 1 and .93/.90/.89 in Phase 2.

They annotated the 27 chapters of the Mandarin translation of The Little Prince manually: after Jieba tokenization, they manually corrected segmentation so potential adpositions were separate tokens, then all annotators jointly identified adposition targets according to their criteria. Supersense labeling was done by three native Mandarin Chinese annotators, with 25% of targets labeled cooperatively and the rest labeled independently by all three and then adjudicated cooperatively, in two phases.
20,287 tokens.

They use a training dataset of about 20,800 news articles with five features: ID, title, author, text, and label. They do not use the provided unlabeled test dataset; instead, they randomly select a test set from the training data for evaluation.

The paper explores three pre-training/vectorization models—TF-IDF, CountVectorizer, and Word2Vec—and five fine-tuning models: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), Long Short-Term Memory networks (LSTMs), Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machine (SVM).

Numerical features, language-model probabilities, word clusters, topic-model representations, part-of-speech tag features, and essay text features are extracted. More specifically, these include 66 numerical features (e.g., counts of sentences/words/letters, Gunning Fog index, difficult/misspelled/duplicate words, low-idf words), probabilities under two trigram language models, binary one-hot word-cluster vectors, concatenated LDA topic distributions, unigram POS bag-of-words, and idf-weighted bigram bag-of-words of the essay text.
The evaluation metrics were the challenge’s official error measure E and accuracy. They also reported stratified 3-fold cross-validation scores and a confusion matrix for error analysis.
The model used is gradient boosted trees with gradient-based one-side sampling, implemented in Microsoft’s Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) toolkit.

Future work is to investigate potential biases in the essay-level prediction process, especially whether LDA topic-distribution features reflect genuine modeling power or dataset artifacts caused by topic information leakage tied to candidate level. The paper suggests further analysis and validation, specifically if essay topics are released so that validation splits can be performed based on topics.
For reference, the baseline was a tuned Logistic Regression with the same class weights for the class imbalance. It achieved error 10.10 and accuracy 97.2 using all features.

Sentence alignment quality is evaluated by manual human evaluation of text alignment using a 5-point scale: 1 = wrong alignment, 2 = partial alignment with slightly compositional translational equivalence, 3 = partial alignment with compositional translation and additional or missing information, 4 = correct alignment with compositional translation and few additional or missing information, and 5 = correct alignment and fully compositional translation. The corpus is split into three bins by hunalign confidence score, and two annotators each rate 30 items from each bin, with 10 shared items for inter-annotator reliability.

Speech alignment quality is evaluated by manual human evaluation of audio-text alignment using a 3-point scale: 1 = wrong alignment, 2 = partial alignment with some words or sentences missing, and 3 = correct alignment allowing non-spoken syllables at the start or end. Two annotators each rated 30 items from each of three bins defined by hunalign confidence-score ranges, with 10 shared items per bin for inter-annotator reliability.

Yes. The paper says the selective gate is designed to handle bags with only one sentence, where selective attention becomes ineffective or useless if that sentence is wrongly labeled, and states that the gate can directly assign a low gating value to wrongly labeled instances to prevent noise propagation.

No. The paper’s “Isolated Sentence in Bag” error analysis says isolated sentences can cause information loss among multiple sentences in bag-level classification, but it does not show an example where only one sentence appears in a bag and their method works better than selective attention.
Unanswerable
SICK (Sentences Involving Compositional Knowledge). They evaluate on its semantic relatedness and textual entailment tasks.

No. They report only end-task results for semantic relatedness and text entailment, while the learned sentence representations are used to generate matching feature planes and then fed to a temporal CNN; there is no separate evaluation of those representations before the CNN layer.
Unanswerable
Unanswerable
The fused system outperformed the others. Among the individual subsystems, eftdnn had the best overall performance.
Yes. The paper states that frequencies of semantically related words and phrases appearing in the same texts should be enhanced, increasing their contribution to topics.

They explore political and economic domains with EuroVoc, and the banking domain with the Russian banking collection and RuThes. They also use general-domain WordNet on English collections including Europarl, JRC-Acquis, ACL Anthology, and NIPS Conference papers.
WordNet, EuroVoc, and RuThes.

They define hate speech as language used to express hatred towards a targeted group or intended to be derogatory, to humiliate, or to insult the members of the group. In extreme cases, it may also be language that threatens or incites violence.
A logistic regression model with L2 regularization in a one-versus-rest framework.
33,458 Twitter users.
Their dataset consists of 85.4 million tweets collected from 33,458 Twitter users, with a manually labeled sample of 24,802 tweets.
SST-2. On this sentiment classification task, not fine-tuning all layers leads to improved quality.
Yes, they test against RoBERTa LARGE. Table 2 reports reproduced development-set results for “RoBERTa LARGE,” and Section 3.1 states that each model has base and large variants.
Unanswerable
Unanswerable

It mitigates the bias by making artifacts in hypotheses balanced across labels via sample reweighting. Specifically, it estimates \(P(y|h)\) with K-fold cross-prediction and trains with sample weights \(w = 1/((1-\epsilon)P(y|h)+\epsilon)\), normalized so label priors remain unchanged.
They use SNLI, MultiNLI, JOCI, and SICK for cross-dataset testing. Specifically, they train on SNLI and MultiNLI and test on SICK and JOCI.

The entropy strategy was the most helpful. For both logistic regression and random forest, it reached optimal performance the quickest, while other strategies showed no clear advantage or were only slightly better than random selection.
Their tweets dataset contained 3,685,984 unique English tweets after integrating the two sources. In total, they collected 5,999,035 tweets, of which 3,685,984 were English.
The IDN Tagged Corpus contains 10K sentences and 250K manually tagged tokens.

The neural network models explored were a feedforward network with a context window and a bidirectional LSTM (biLSTM). For prediction, they also tested both a softmax layer with greedy decoding and a CRF layer with Viterbi decoding.

The rule-based model evaluated was the rule-based tagger designed by Rashel et al. It tags named entities and multi-word expressions using a dictionary, uses MorphInd to tag remaining words, and applies 15 hand-crafted post-processing rules to resolve ambiguous tags.
The task uses the IDN Tagged Corpus. It contains 10K sentences and 250K manually tagged tokens.
They use parts of Chinese Wikipedia articles, yielding 11,529,432 segmented words for training. They also collect stroke information for 20,402 characters.
No. They evaluate the embeddings only on two test tasks: word similarity and word analogy.
Unanswerable

The issues identified were that the model lacked flexibility for out-of-vocabulary words and, for unknown words, it assigned a zero vector, which harmed test results. The paper also notes this lack of flexibility could sometimes be seen as an advantage because the model recognized the mismatch of a wrong word with its class.
Yes. Section 3.4 reports evaluation results for “POS and morph classes,” including precision, recall, and F1 scores.
It extends standard ENAMEX by adding a fourth named-entity class, facility (FAC), to the usual organization, location, and person types.
Gender, number, and case.

Yes. The reported results are on a held-out test set of Twitter users from a corpus of publicly available tweets by users who announced their pregnancy on Twitter, and no non-English datasets are mentioned.

The dataset is a sample of 10,417 Twitter users drawn from a database of more than 400 million publicly available tweets by over 100,000 users who had announced their pregnancy on Twitter. Two professional annotators labeled users as “bot,” “non-bot,” or “unavailable” based on their publicly available Twitter sites, with κ = 0.93 inter-annotator agreement on 1,000 overlapping annotations. After resolving disagreements, 413 users (4%) were bots, 7,849 (75.35%) were non-bots, and 2,069 (19.9%) were unavailable.

By extending a political bot detector like Botometer and retraining it for a health-related cohort using annotated health-related Twitter users. The paper customizes it by using the Botometer score as a feature in a gradient boosting classifier, tuning the bot threshold, addressing class imbalance with SMOTE, and adding health-relevant features such as tweet diversity, URL frequency, posting frequency, topic heterogeneity, post length, profile-picture face detection, and person-name detection in usernames.

Bot detection research takes place in social media for health-related tasks. Specifically, the paper discusses using social media data for health-related research and customizing automatic bot detection for users posting health-related information on Twitter.
Yes. The QA tuples come from a customer support dataset sourced from Twitter.
The baseline model is bert-base-multilingual-cased without emoji tokens. It is compared against bert-base-multilingual-cased with emoji tokens.
2000 tuples.
A customer support dataset of 2000 question-answer tuples sourced from Twitter, collected by Hori and Hori.

They used boosting via a Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM), described as fast gradient boosting on decision trees. The boosting framework combined predictions from the base classifiers with TF-IDF and QIEF input features.
Yes. They experimented with two pretrained text embeddings: the original BERT (Bert-Base) and BioBERT.
96,328 sequences.

The new dataset, PICONET, was collected from PubMed structured abstracts by searching on April 11, 2019 for 363,078 English human clinical-trial abstracts and selecting section headings that specifically represented Population/Problem, Intervention, and Outcome. The authors lemmatized section labels to group similar headings, manually inspected samples to choose suitable labels, kept full abstract sections instead of splitting them into sentences, created multi-label examples for sections like “population and intervention,” added negative examples from headings such as “aim,” and cleaned the data with language identification, removal of missing values, unicode cleaning, and filtering sequences to 5–200 words.
Unanswerable

Previous datasets are described as having redundancy and ambiguity. Specifically, the paper criticizes keyword-based labeling because sections like “population and methods” are not purely about one PIO category and may mix in interventions or study design, and it notes that in Jin and Szolovits (2018), multi-sentence sections labeled as P were split into individual P sentences, which can produce poor candidates and lose feature-rich context.
Unanswerable

They explore all four agreement relation types: subject-verb agreement, noun-determiner agreement, noun-attributive adjective agreement, and subject-predicate adjective agreement. They study these across the features number, gender, case, and person.
Amazon, Yelp, IMDB, MR, MPQA, Subj, and TREC.
Their approach is compared against the TextFooler baseline. It is evaluated on three text classification models: word-LSTM, word-CNN, and a fine-tuned BERT base-uncased classifier.
0.008 F1 points.
Buschmeier, Cimiano, and Klinger (2014), with P=.82, R=.69, and F1=.744.
The baseline models are All Sarcasm, which assumes every instance is sarcastic, and Random, which randomly assigns each instance as sarcastic or non-sarcastic.
Twitter and Amazon product reviews.

The training data came from two domains: Twitter and Amazon product reviews. Amazon reviews were from Filatova’s (2012) publicly available sarcasm corpus, and the Twitter set was built from tweets collected in February–March 2016 containing exactly one trailing hashtag, with #sarcasm labeled sarcastic and #happiness, #sadness, #anger, #surprise, #fear, and #disgust labeled non-sarcastic. Both datasets were split randomly into 80% training and 20% testing, yielding Twitter train counts of 1568 sarcastic and 2430 non-sarcastic, and Amazon train counts of 350 sarcastic and 653 non-sarcastic.
The best model is LSTM and GRU with Attention, with a macro-F1 of 0.89. Its weighted average precision, recall, and F1 are 0.93, 0.88, and 0.88, respectively.

Two SVMs (linear and RBF), Stochastic Gradient Descent classifier, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, Bernoulli Naïve Bayes, Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT.

LSTM and GRU with Attention best performs on the offensive language identification task. It outperformed all other models in macro-F1 and in precision, recall, and F1 for both the Offensive and Not Offensive classes.
No. The annotations were done by a team of three volunteers using LightTag.
3 volunteers.
No. The dataset contained over 29% offensive content, with 1,401 offensive vs. 3,378 not offensive tweets.

They experiment on five classical machine learning models—linear SVM, RBF SVM, SGDC, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes—and six deep learning models: Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT.
No. The document describes two separate datasets of hotel reviews in Catalan and Basque, but does not indicate that any reviews are translations available in both languages.
910 reviews total: 567 Catalan and 343 Basque.
3 annotators.

It identifies the relationship between sentence-structure elements and word chunks using POS tags and a dependency-based parse tree. First, program Π2 finds the main components—subject, verb, and object—based on the recognized sentence structure and Universal Dependency core arguments, with special handling for “to be” constructions; then program Π3 recursively finds complement components such as compound nouns, adjective modifiers, conjunctions, prepositional phrases, and adverbial modifiers from non-core and nominal dependency relations. The output is a list of vocabularies for the next steps.

The two use cases are: generating natural language descriptions for annotated ontologies/workflows, including the Phylotastic project and a people ontology; and creating an intermediate representation for Wikipedia pages that can be translated and regenerated, evaluated on articles from People, Mathematics, and Food & Drink.
Yes. They explore how their continuous language vectors vary across languages, showing that the vectors capture similarity and genetic relationships between languages via clustering analyses.

A standard stacked character-based LSTM with two layers, followed by a hidden layer and a softmax output layer. They concatenate language embedding vectors to the LSTM inputs at each time step and to the hidden layer before the softmax.

They show genetic relationships by hierarchically clustering the learned language vectors, illustrated for a subset of Germanic languages in Figure 5. This clustering closely matches the established genetic relationships within that language family.

No. They tested MagiCoder by automatically comparing its encodings with previously manually revised and validated ADR reports, and the conclusion that it reduces the time required for encoding ADR reports is presented as a practical point, not as a test on real pharmacologists.

Yes. In the discussion, the authors hypothesize that this is because ADR reports are generally badly or hurriedly written and do not have a complex structure, which they empirically observed also for long descriptions.

The MagiCoder algorithm has five phases: definition of ad hoc data structures, preprocessing of the original ADR description, word-by-word linear scan of the description with a voting task, weight calculation, and selection/ordering/release of winning terms. The preprocessing includes tokenization, stemming, and removal of irrelevant words; the scan lets each word vote for LLTs it belongs to and stores matching information; then recognized terms are weighted based on syntactical matching. Finally, voted terms are pruned, sorted, and a set of winning MedDRA terms is released.

The system is made linear by preprocessing MedDRA into hash-table meta-dictionaries (`DictByWord` and `DictByWordStem`) in \(O(km)\), where each word maps directly to the set of LLT terms containing it, so checking whether a narrative token appears in the terminology is \(O(1)\). Then the algorithm performs a word-by-word scan of the narrative and updates auxiliary hash-based structures in constant time, avoiding approximate string matching because it would prevent direct dictionary access and hurt efficiency.

They conclude that personal recovery in bipolar disorder involves living a satisfying, hopeful, and contributing life despite illness-related limitations, and they note the commonly used CHIME aspects: Connectedness, Hope and optimism, Identity, Meaning and purpose, and Empowerment. They argue that analyzing large-scale first-person social media accounts can provide a more complete picture of the aspects and mechanisms of recovery, including factors that facilitate or hinder it, especially across more heterogeneous, multilingual, and culturally diverse populations than prior structured Western studies.
Unanswerable

The analysis used a mixed-methods, explanatory sequential design starting with statistical analysis of the full dataset and followed by manual investigation of selected examples. The computational linguistic methods mentioned include comparative corpus analysis with Wmatrix for keywords and key concepts, an iterative recovery-term dictionary with automatic semantic tagging using USAS, distributional semantics for co-occurring terms, unsupervised topic modelling with DLATK, and emotion and sentiment analysis using supervised machine learning and multilingual embeddings.
No. The paper states that they will not ask the Twitter and Reddit account holders included in the datasets for their consent.

Twitter and Reddit users with bipolar disorder will be identified automatically via self-reported diagnosis statements, such as “I was diagnosed with BD-I last week.” Relevant blogs will be manually identified, and their authors will be contacted to obtain informed consent for using their texts.
The training/testing data comes from CCPC1.0. For CI, they focus on the top frequent 121 forms, which constitute 80% of CCPC1.0.
SHI and CI. Specifically, the generated major types are SHI forms Jueju and Lvshi, including Wuyan Jueju, Qiyan Jueju, Wuyan Lvshi, and Qiyan Lvshi, as well as 121 major forms (Cipai) of CI.

They compare mainly against BPE-based subword NMT (Sennrich, Haddow, and Birch, 2015b) and a fully character-based NMT baseline from Lee, Cho, and Hofmann (2016). In related work, they also position against prior morphology-aware MT/NMT methods including Luong and Manning (2016), Jean et al. (2014), Mi, Wang, and Ittycheriah (2016), Sennrich and Haddow (2016), and Tamchyna, Marco, and Fraser (2017).
Yes. They use the word2vec tool and train both SG (skip-gram) and CBOW models.

Quality of the word vectors is measured by evaluation tasks: word similarity, synonym selection, and word analogy. In the word similarity task specifically, it is measured by the Pearson correlation coefficient between human word-similarity scores and cosine distances between word vectors, where a higher coefficient indicates better word vectors.
No. They report results on multiple datasets, including KPTimes and JPTimes news articles, as well as KPCrowd, DUC-2001, SemEval, and KP20k.

The news texts come primarily from The New York Times, collected by crawling selected online news websites and retrieving free-to-read articles from 2006 to 2017 via the Internet Archive. A secondary source is the Japan Times.
FirstPhrase is used as the baseline for this task. It extracts the first N keyphrase candidates from a document.

Two neural keyphrase generation models were trained: CopySci and CopyNews. They use the same CopyRNN encoder-decoder architecture with a copying mechanism, with CopySci trained on scientific abstracts and CopyNews trained on newspaper articles.

Editors’ annotations are more uniform and consistent than those in existing datasets, using a more controlled indexing vocabulary with frequent keyphrases close to topic descriptors rather than the larger, less controlled vocabularies used by non-expert annotators. They are also more likely to generalize by assigning keyphrases that do not occur in the document, with a higher proportion of missing keyphrases (about 55%).
Unanswerable
Yes. They use word embeddings learned through the unsupervised GloVe approach for the context words.
The model has one hidden layer.
F-measure is the metric they use, reported as F-measure(%). They also mention accuracy in terms of F-measure.
The datasets used were DUC-2001, Inspec, the NUS Keyphrase Corpus, and the ICSI Meeting Corpus.
Their model’s F1 score was 27.53 on DUC, 27.01 on Inspec, 4.30 on ICSI, and 9.10 on NUS.
The paper identifies SingleRank and Topical PageRank as the state-of-the-art models used for comparison. It states that WikiRank improves over these models by more than 2% in F1-score.

The paper does not verify word alignments against gold annotations. It uses the encoder-decoder attention distributions of a well-trained ART teacher model as word alignment information and trains the NART student to match them via KL-divergence, based on the observation that ART attentions are less ambiguous.

The paper says the autoregressive translation model suffers from high inference latency because it must generate tokens sequentially during decoding. It also states that the proposed non-autoregressive model is one order of magnitude faster in inference than a strong LSTM-based ART baseline.
BLEU is used to measure translation accuracy.
Yes. Besides WMT14, they also used the IWSLT14 German-to-English dataset.
Yes. The experimental results are reported not only for WMT14 German-English/English-German but also for IWSLT14 German-English, so the results apply beyond just one German-English direction.

The most discriminating features are average, maximum, minimum, and standard deviation, with average-based features having the highest information gain. Probability and proportionality features have low information gain.

On the alternate dataset, the DSLCC development set, LDR, Skip-gram, and SenVec obtain comparable results, supporting the robustness of LDR. The accuracies are: Bulgarian 99.9/100/100, Macedonian 99.9/100/100, Spain Spanish 84.7/82.1/86.3, Argentina Spanish 88.0/90.3/87.6, Portugal Portuguese 87.4/83.2/90.0, Brazilian Portuguese 90.0/94.5/87.6, Bosnian 78.0/80.3/74.4, Croatian 85.8/85.9/84.7, Serbian 86.4/75.1/91.2, Indonesian 99.4/99.3/99.4, Malay 99.2/99.2/99.8, Czech 99.8/99.9/99.8, Slovak 99.3/100/99.3, and Other languages 99.9/99.8/99.8.

No. For BioASQ, the paper says answer spans are extracted by searching for occurrences of the gold standard answer in the snippets, but this is imperfect and can yield false negatives when “the exact string used is not in the synonym list”; no numeric coverage is reported.
Unanswerable

SGNN is used for script event prediction, with the paper replacing SGNN’s input event embeddings by their intent- and sentiment-enhanced event embeddings. For stock market prediction, the document does not specify a single prediction method used by their model.

It is evaluated on the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset, where given an existing event context, the model chooses the most reasonable subsequent event from a candidate list. The paper follows Li et al. (2018a) and uses the SGNN framework, replacing its input event embeddings with the proposed intent- and sentiment-enhanced event embeddings.

The paper uses the hard similarity dataset from Weber et al. (2018) with 230 event pairs and an extended version annotated by the authors with 1,000 event pairs, the transitive sentence similarity dataset from Kartsaklis and Sadrzadeh (2014) with 108 pairs of transitive sentences, and the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset for script event prediction. For stock market prediction, the paper evaluates on S&P 500 index prediction.
No. In the experiments, they compare against SGNS, PPMI-SVD, a Char2Vec variant without attention, Porter Stemmer, and Morfessor, but not fastText or another subword-information model.
Unanswerable
English.

The embeddings are evaluated by ranking word pairs according to the cosine similarity of their word vectors and then measuring the correlation with human similarity judgments using Spearman’s ρ. The comparison uses the WordSim353 dataset, the MEN test split, and the Rare Word dataset.
15.6/16.5 (accuracy/NDCG) on MCTest-150 and 7.3/4.6 on MCTest-500.

They compared to Addition, Addition-proj, NR (the Neural Reasoner), and AR (the Attentive Reader). NR and AR are described as top-performing deep neural networks for QA tasks, while Addition and Addition-proj are simpler baselines without complex composition and inference.
The baseline is a one-stage RNN system. Its RNN structure is the same as the last stage, containing a 2-layer BLSTM, and it is directly trained to recognize dialect category.

The two-stage system achieves the best results: 88.88% ACC overall, 87.72% for ≤3s, and 90.04% for >3s, compared with the baseline’s 78.85%, 77.60%, and 80.10% and the three-stage system’s 87.24%, 85.52%, and 88.96%. They report roughly a 10% relative accuracy improvement over the baseline on every task, with especially strong performance on long utterances and high dialect-specific accuracies such as Shanghai 98.8% and Hefei 99.8%.
Ningxia, Hefei, Sichuan, Shanxi, Changsha, Hebei, Nanchang, Shanghai, Kekka, and Fujian.
Transformer architecture.
Unanswerable
The restricted track system does not use any external NLP tools, spell checkers, or hand-crafted confusion sets, and it relies solely on neural sequence models.
Unanswerable
There is no clear winner: MODULAR is better at reducing bias, while CONCURRENT better preserves meaning and fluency.

The edits were a Wiki community effort: the corpus consists of sentences “pre and post-neutralization by English Wikipedia editors.” Unanswerable whether annotators were trained to do it, because the document only describes Wikipedia editors making the edits.

Subjective text is automatically neutralized by translating a biased source sentence into a target sentence that preserves meaning while removing subjective bias. The paper proposes two neural approaches: a MODULAR system that first detects biased words with a BERT-based tagger and then edits the sentence with an LSTM encoder-decoder, and a CONCURRENT system that directly generates a neutralized sentence with a BERT encoder and LSTM decoder.
The task investigated is sign language recognition using multimodal data, specifically skeletal and RGB video data. It is evaluated on a large-scale American Sign Language dataset with 51 signs.
The best model is Spatial AI-LSTM with 81% cross-subject accuracy.
